segments.s317-g49: همانگه (an adverb) = 'at that moment'; it sets the time for the clause. نشستند is a past plural verb meaning 'they sat' or here 'they mounted' (نشستنِ بر اسب = to mount a horse). The normal subject شاهان (kings) follows the verb in the line for poetic word order but is the logical subject: 'the kings sat (mounted) on horses.' بر اسب is the prepositional phrase 'on/onto horses' describing where they sat/mounted.
segments.s317-g50: برفتند is a past plural verb 'they departed/rode off' (بر + رفتند, poetic compound). ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. ایوان is 'iwan/portico' (the place they left). آذر گشسب is a personal name (two words forming the name Azar‑Gushasp) that follows the place noun to mark whose iwan it is; together the phrase means 'from the iwan of Azar‑Gushasp.'
segments.s317-g51: پراندیشه شد is a two‑word predicate: پراندیشه (an adjective/description) 'pensive/thoughtful' + شد 'became' yields 'became pensive.' زآن سخن is the prepositional phrase 'because of that speech' (زآن = 'from/because of that', سخن = 'speech/words') giving the cause. شهریار at the end is the subject 'the king' — word order here is flexible/poetic with the subject placed after the cause phrase.
segments.s317-g52: بیامد is a past simple verb 'he came' (used here as intransitive, resumed action). به نزدیک is a locative/goal phrase 'to near' or 'toward the near (place/person)'; پرهیزگار is the noun 'the pious one' (an epithet) that functions as the object of approach: 'he came close to the pious (man).' The omitted pronoun subject (he) refers back to شهریار in the previous segment.
segments.s317-g53: چو here means 'when'; it introduces a temporal subordinate clause. هوم is the proper name 'Hom' (the subject). آن سر و تاج شاهان is a nominal phrase 'that head and crown of the kings' (آن = 'that', سر = 'head', و تاج = 'and crown', شاهان = 'of the kings'); its word order places the modifiers before the verb. بدید is the simple past 'he saw' (ب‑ prefix plus دیدن in poetic register).
segments.s317-g54: بر ایشان = 'upon them' (preposition بر + pronoun ایشان). به داد آفرین is a compound accusative/phrase: به داد = 'to/for (their) justice' or 'in praise of justice', and آفرین here means the acclamation 'praise/bravo.' گسترید is a past (poetic) verb 'he spread/poured forth' (گستردن); together the clause says he 'poured forth the acclamation (in honor) of justice upon them.'
segments.s317-g55: همه شهریاران بر او آفرین: "همه شهریاران" is the subject phrase 'all the kings/ladies', followed by the prepositional phrase "بر او" ('to/upon him') indicating the target of the exclamation. "آفرین" is an exclamatory noun ('praise', 'bravo') placed without an immediate finite verb here; the verb is supplied by the next segment. Word order is typical of poetry: subject → prepositional target → exclamation.
segments.s317-g56: همی خواندند از جهان‌آفرین: "همی + خواندند" is a poetic past form meaning a repeated or continuous past action ('they kept chanting/singing'). "خواندند" here means 'sang/chan ted out' (they produced the exclamation). "از جهان‌آفرین" gives the content/target of the chant — literally 'concerning/of the World‑Creator', i.e. they chanted praise referring to the Creator of the world. The clause supplies the verb that completes the previous segment's elliptical exclamation.
segments.s317-g41: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing the temporal clause. گودرز is the subject (proper name). بشنید is a past tense verb 'he heard' (classical past form). این داستان is the object 'this story' placed after the verb in the clause.
segments.s317-g42: بیاد آمدش is a fixed idiom 'came to his mind' (بیاد = 'to/into memory', آمدش = 'it came to him'). گفته functions as a noun 'statement/report' and راستان qualifies it as 'righteous/true' (an adjectival use), so the phrase means 'the true/righteous saying came to him.'
segments.s317-g43: از آنجا is a prepositional phrase 'from there'. بشد is an archaic/poetic past-form (from شدن) meaning 'it happened / he went / he turned' here indicating movement that took place. سوی آتشکده is the directional complement 'toward the fire‑temple'.
segments.s317-g44: چنانچون بود is 'just as/for' + past copula 'was', introducing the reason or circumstance. مردم is 'the people/folk' and دلشده is a predicate adjective 'distraught/troubled' describing them; together 'as the people were distraught.'
segments.s317-g45: نخستین is an adverb/adjective 'first' marking sequence. بر آتش here marks the recipient/target 'to the fire' (classical usage). ستایش گرفت is a verbal phrase 'took/received praise' idiomatically 'offered praise' — گرفت is the past tense 'took/received' attached to the noun to form the verbal idiom.
segments.s317-g46: جهان‌آفرین را marks the definite object 'the Creator' with the object marker را. نیایش گرفت is the parallel verbal idiom 'he took/received prayer' meaning 'he offered a prayer' — again گرفت is the past tense verb completing the verbal noun نیایش.
segments.s317-g47: بپردخت is the past of پرداختن here meaning 'he set about / applied himself' (classical past prefixed with ب-). و is the coordinating 'and'. بگشاد is the past of گشودن 'he opened/revealed'. راز از نهفت is 'the secret from concealment' = 'the hidden secret' (راز object, از نهفت a prepositional phrase 'from hiding').
segments.s317-g48: همان دیده uses همان 'that/the very' and دیده as a past-participial noun 'that which was seen' (i.e. 'what he had seen'). بر شهریاران بگفت is 'he said/told to the kings' — بر used here with an indirect object meaning 'to', and بگفت is the past-tense 'he told'.
segments.s317-g1: چنان بد که is a fixed classical pattern meaning “so (it was) that / in such a way that”; چنان (so) + بد (here adverbial/qualifying) introduce the following clause. گودرز کشوادگان is an appositive noun phrase: the proper name گودرز followed by an epithet/tribal/office name کشوادگان; there is no copula — the phrase functions as the subject of the clause introduced by که.
segments.s317-g2: همی رفت is a classical/perfective-past form with همی + past verb giving an imperfective or past-continuous sense (“was going” / habitual past). با + NP (با گیو و آزادگان) is the prepositional complement “with Giv and the آزادگان”; word order here places the verb before the PP, a normal option in Persian poetry.
segments.s317-g3: گرازان و پویان is a coordinated subject phrase (“boars and pursuers/seekers”); پویان here functions as plural agent ‘those who seek’ (i.e. pursuers). به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase meaning “toward/near the king” (به + نزدیک + noun).
segments.s317-g4: به دریا درون کرد is a light-verb construction: درون (inward / into) + کردن (کرد) with به دریا (“into/toward the sea”) — here meaning “turned/laid (their) gaze into the sea” or “looked toward the sea.” چندی نگاه (literally “a short while’s look”) indicates duration: چندی modifies نگاه and yields “looked for a while.”
segments.s317-g5: به چشم آمدش is an experiential construction: به چشم (to the eye) + آمد + enclitic pronoun ش (to him) = “appeared to him” / “came into his sight.” هوم با آن کمند follows as the visible entity: the name هوم (here a proper name) with the instrument/possession phrase با آن کمند (“with that lasso”).
segments.s317-g6: نوان is a noun meaning “a youth / young one.” بر لب آب is a locative phrase: بر + لب آب = “on the brink/edge of the water.” The second بر before مستمند links toward/against/at the beneficiary/target (مستمند “the needy”), so this short string describes the youth’s position and his relation (direction/aim) toward the needy; Persian poetry often omits an explicit verb here, leaving the participial/nominal phrases to be read with the previous verb (آمد) or with understood action.
segments.s317-g7: همان گونه is an adverbial phrase meaning “in the same way / likewise.” آب را تیره دید is a canonical Persian object–predicate–verb sequence with را marking the definite object (آب را) and تیره as an object complement/adjective: “he saw the water dark.” دید is the past tense verb “saw.”
segments.s317-g8: پرستنده را is another definite object (“the worshipper”). دیدگان خیره is a noun phrase (دیدگان = eyes) + adjective (خیره = staring) placed before the final verb دید; the whole phrase means roughly “he saw the worshipper, (and) the eyes (were) staring” — the adjective modifies دیدگان, and the final دید supplies the seeing action for the whole phrase.
segments.s317-g113: فغان کرد is a verbal phrase (فغان کردن) in the simple past: "he lamented/wailed." The following clause is a direct vocative (address) introduced by کای, an archaic vocative "O," so کای شهریار جهان is best read as "O king of the world" and is what the speaker cries out.
segments.s317-g114: This is a continuation of the vocative: two epithets in apposition to the addressee. سر here means "chief/head" (an epithet), نامداران is an attributive plural "the renowned," and تاج مهان gives another epithet "crown of the great/guests." The nouns are coordinated (سر ... و تاج ...) and modify the vocative before.
segments.s317-g115: کجات is an archaic interrogative meaning "where?/whence?" placed at the front for emphasis; there is no explicit verb—classical Persian often omits the copula in rhetorical questions. آن همه pairs the demonstrative "that" with the intensifier "all/so much," and the sequence رسم و آیین و گاه lists "custom, ritual, and occasion/time."
segments.s317-g116: The interrogative کجات is repeated to ask about another set of things. آن marks the things asked about; سر تاج are two coordinated titles ("head/chief and crown"), and چنان (here written چندان) modifies سپاه as "so many / such a large" (i.e. a great host/army). The structure is a string of nouns linked by و without an overt verb.
segments.s317-g117: Again کجات introduces a rhetorical question "whence?" آن همه emphasizes the quantity "all that." دانش is "knowledge/learning," و links زور ("strength/might") and دست (here "hand" used idiomatically as "power/force of the hand"); the three nouns form a coordinated list describing capacities or virtues.
segments.s317-g118: The second question repeats کجات and asks about people: آن بزرگان "those great ones" (nobles or magnates). خسروپرست is a compound adjective "Khosrow-worshipping" or "devoted to the king," qualifying بزرگان. The phrase asks rhetorically where such nobles came from.
segments.s317-g119: کجات ان introduces another rhetorical "whence?" phrase. به رزم اندرون is an adverbial phrase: به رزم "in/at battle" with اندرون "within/inside" emphasizing location. فر (royal glory, farr) and نام (name/fame) are the qualities produced "in battle," coordinated by و; classical Persian often strings such noun phrases without an explicit verb.
segments.s317-g120: The pattern is repeated for the courtly/banquet context: به بزم اندرون "within the banquet/feast." کام (desire/fulfillment/pleasure) and جام (cup/wine-cup) are coordinated objects associated with the bāzм (banquet). The sentence contrasts battlefield glory with banquetly pleasure by parallel structure.
segments.s317-g105: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when') and is followed by the past verb بشنید ('he heard'); آوازش is the object ('his voice') marked by the possessive -ش, and افراسیاب (the subject) appears after the verb in the typical older/poetic word order (verb before subject).
segments.s317-g106: پر از درد is a prepositional phrase meaning 'full of pain/sorrow' (پر + از + noun); گریان is a predicate/adjectival participle 'weeping' that describes the subject; برآمد is a past tense verb 'arose/rose', and ز (poetic form of از) + آب gives the source/location 'from the water'.
segments.s317-g107: به دریا is a directional prepositional phrase 'toward/into the sea'; همی is the archaic/progressive particle indicating repeated or continuous action ('kept/was'), کرد is the verb 'did/made' used here in the idiom پای کردن ('to set foot'), and آشناه (subject) follows the verb as 'the companion/familiar'.
segments.s317-g108: بیامد is a past tense verb 'he came' with the subject understood from the previous clause; به جایی 'to a place' introduces the location, and که introduces a relative clause modifying جایی — بد پایگاه is a nominal phrase 'a bad/ill footing or base' describing that place.
segments.s317-g109: ز خشکی uses the poetic z (ز) meaning 'from' + خشکی 'the dry land/shore'; چو introduces the temporal clause 'when'; بانگ برادر is a possessive/genitive phrase 'the brother's cry' and شنید is the past verb 'he heard'.
segments.s317-g110: بر او بدتر آمد is an idiomatic expression meaning 'it came worse upon him' (بر used with a person + آمد 'befell/happened'); ز مرگ آنچه دید places the clause 'that which he saw' in comparison to death ('worse than death'), where آنچه introduces the subordinate clause whose verb is دید 'he saw'.
segments.s317-g111: چو introduces the temporal clause ('when'); گرسیوز is the subject; او را is the object marked by the accusative/topic marker را; بدید is the past verb 'saw'; اندر آب is the locative phrase 'in the water' (اندر is a poetic form of 'in').
segments.s317-g112: دو دیده پر از خون is a descriptive phrase 'two eyes full of blood' where پر از + noun means 'full of ...'; و links the coordinated description, and دل پر شتاب 'heart full of haste' uses the same 'پر' (= full) construction (here without the explicit از before شتاب), so both noun phrases describe the subject's physical/emotional state.
segments.s317-g9: به دل گفت is an idiom meaning 'he said in his heart' (i.e., he thought to himself): به (preposition) + دل (object 'heart') followed by گفت (past tense 'said'). کاین is the fused poetic form of 'که این' (= 'that this'), introducing the content of his thought; مرد پرهیزگار is a noun phrase 'this pious man' (noun + adjective).
segments.s317-g10: ز دریای 'from the sea' (ز + دریای). چیچست is an archaic fused interrogative (چی + چیست) = 'what is it?' and queries the object of taking. گیرد شکار is an agent-verb-object unit where گیرد (3rd sg. present/habitual 'takes/seizes') governs شکار ('prey/quarry'); poetic word order places the verb before its object.
segments.s317-g11: نهنگی is an indefinite/qualifying subject 'a whale' (the -ی ending often gives an indefinite sense). مگر here introduces a supposition ('perhaps'/'maybe'). دم ماهی is a genitive phrase 'the tail of the fish' (دم = 'tip/tail'), and گرفت is the past tense 'seized' or 'took'. Word order is normal subject – (adverb) – object – verb.
segments.s317-g12: به دیدار از او is a prepositional phrase 'at the sight of him' (به + دیدار used idiomatically, از marking the object 'him'). مانده is a participial/past adjective 'remained/was left', and اندر شگفت ('in wonder') uses اندر as a locative preposition plus شگفت as a noun 'wonder/astonishment'.
segments.s317-g13: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (به + او contracted to بدو), with گفت as the reporting verb. کای is a vocative particle ('O' addressing someone), followed by مرد پرهیزگار 'pious man' as the addressee.
segments.s317-g14: نهانی functions as 'secret/hidden' (here introducing the idea of something concealed). چه داری is the interrogative clause 'what do you have/possess?' (چه = what, داری = 2nd/3rd-singular present 'you/he have'). بکن آشکار is an imperative construction: بکن (do/make) + آشکار ('manifest/clear'), i.e. 'reveal it' or 'make it evident.'
segments.s317-g15: از این آب دریا is a poetic ordering meaning 'from this sea (of water)' (از = from; این آب دریا = 'this water, the sea' or 'this sea of water'). چه جویی همی is the interrogative 'what are you seeking?' where جویی is the present form of جُستن ('seek') and همی is an archaic particle marking present/habitual action.
segments.s317-g16: مگر here introduces a rhetorical/expectant question ('are you perhaps...'). تیره تن را is the object 'the dark/blackened body' (تیره = dark; تن = body; را = definite object marker). بشویی همی is present-tense 'you wash' (بشویی = 'you wash', همی again marking ongoing/habitual), so the clause reads 'are you perhaps washing/wishing to wash your dark body?'.
segments.s317-g25: همانگه is an adverb meaning 'at that moment' (temporal adverb). گمان برد is a verbal phrase: گمان 'a thought/feeling' + برد (past of بردن here) meaning 'took/ seized', so literally 'a thought took (possession of)'. روشن دلم is an adjective + noun with the possessive enclitic: روشن 'clear/manifest' modifies دل 'heart', and ـم attached to دل (دلم) is the first-person possessive 'my'. Word order (adjective before noun) and the enclitic show 'my heart became clear with the thought.'
segments.s317-g26: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that ...'. من is the 1st-person subject 'I'. بیخ کین is a genitive construction: بیخ 'root' + کین 'of hatred/vengeance' (the modifier follows the head). از جهان is a prepositional phrase 'from the world'. بگسلم is formed with the prefix بـ + the verb گسلم (from گسستن 'to break/sever'); here it expresses the speaker's intent/result ('I will/should sever' or 'I would uproot').
segments.s317-g27: بدین گونه is a frozen adverbial phrase meaning 'in this manner/so'; formally it builds from the prepositional/prefix element (بـ) + an index 'this' and گونه 'manner'. آواز is the noun 'sound/voice/call'. هنگام خواب is a time phrase 'at the time of sleep/at bedtime' (هنگام 'at the time of' + خواب 'sleep').
segments.s317-g28: نشاید is an impersonal negative construction meaning 'it is not fitting/it should not be'. که introduces the content clause 'that it be ...'. باشد is the substantive verb in subjunctive/optative sense 'be' (here following نشاید). جز is the exception particle 'except', and افراسیاب is the proper name serving as the only plausible agent: 'it cannot be anyone except Afrasiab.'
segments.s317-g29: به + infinitive (جستن) forms a verbal-noun phrase 'to search/for searching', and گرفتم (past of گرفتن) means 'I took/ seized' — together meaning 'I set about searching' or 'I searched / I undertook to search'. همه modifies the following nouns 'کوه و غار' ('all the mountains and caves'), with the coordinating و 'and' between them.
segments.s317-g30: بدیدم is simple past 'I saw'. در هنگ is a locative/temporal phrase 'in/at the time' (در 'in' + هنگ 'moment/time'). آن is the demonstrative 'that' modifying سوگوار, which here functions as a noun 'the mourner' or as an adjective substantivized ('the mourning one'). The phrase thus means 'I saw, in that moment, the mourner.'
segments.s317-g31: دو دستش = 'his two hands' (دو numeral + noun + enclitic ش = 'his'). به زنار بستم is 'I bound (بستم, past of بستن) with/to a girdle' where به + noun indicates the instrument/means 'with a girdle' or 'to a girdle'. چو سنگ is a simile 'like stone' (چو = 'as/like' + سنگ 'stone'), describing the firmness/rigidity of the binding.
segments.s317-g32: بدان سان 'in that manner/so' introduces the result clause. که introduces the content/result 'that ...'. خونریز is an adjective 'blood‑shedding / bloody'. بودش is the past form of بودن with the enclitic pronoun (بود + ـش) used here as 'were/was' referring back to the claws; the verb precedes the noun for emphasis. دو چنگ is the subject 'two claws' (چنگ = claw, talon). The line means 'in such a way that his two claws were bloody.'
segments.s317-g81: "به بند کمندش ببستم چو سنگ" is built around the prepositional phrase به بند کمندش (lit. “to/onto the bond of his snare”) followed by the 1st-person past verb ببستم ("I tied/fastened"). The short simile چو سنگ ("like/as a stone") is appended and links directly into the following verb phrase: it qualifies the manner in which the subject acts (i.e. he was handled as if he were a stone). Word order is flexible and poetic: the prepositional phrase precedes the verb for emphasis.
segments.s317-g82: کشیدمش is a compact past form meaning "I pulled/drew him": کشیدم (I pulled) + enclitic کم (ـش) = "him". بیچاره is an inserted appositive or vocative modifying that object ("the poor wretch"). زآن جای تنگ is a prepositional phrase "from that narrow place" (زآن = "from that", جای = "place", تنگ = "narrow").
segments.s317-g83: به خواهش بدو is an adverbial phrase meaning "at his request" (به = "at/to", خواهش = "request", بدو = "to him"). سست کردم کمند is the verb phrase "I made (it) lax/loosened the snare" where سست کردن is used causatively/denotatively and کردم is the 1st-person past "I did/caused"; کمند is the direct object (word order places the object after the verb here).
segments.s317-g84: چو آمد بر آب is a temporal clause "when/when it came upon the water" (چو = "when/as", آمد = "came", بر = "on/to", آب = "water"). بگشاد بند is a short result clause "the fetter opened/was loosened": بگشاد (perfective/preterite form "opened/was loosed") and بند (the fetter) follows as the affected item; the structure implies an intransitive or passive sense—the bond gave way when it reached the water.
segments.s317-g85: به آب اندرست این زمان ناپدید: "به آب" is a locative phrase ('in/into the water') with به used poetically for location. "اندرست" is an archaic fused form meaning 'is within' (third singular), so the verb 'is' is encoded in this word. "این زمان ناپدید" is a temporal + predicate phrase ('now vanished'), with the time phrase preceding the adjective; together they state his present condition — presently gone into the water.
segments.s317-g86: پی او ز گیتی بباید برید: "پی او" ('after him', 'following him') introduces whose fate is at issue. "ز گیتی" ('from the world') uses poetic ز = 'from'. "بباید برید" is an impersonal necessity construction: "بباید" = 'it is necessary / one must' and "برید" (here infinitival/oblique sense 'to depart/be cut off') expresses the required action — so the clause means 'whoever follows him must be cut off from / leave the world.' The subject is indefinite/understood (those who follow).
segments.s317-g87: ورا‌گر برد باز گیرد سپهر: "وراگر" ('and if' / 'but if') introduces a conditional. "برد" is used as a noun 'victory' (not a finite verb here). "باز" ('again') modifies the verb that follows. "گیرد" is third‑person singular present/future 'seizes/takes', and "سپهر" ('the sky/heavens') is the subject placed after the verb in inverted, poetic order. Literally: 'and if victory again takes the heavens' → idiomatically, 'if victory is regained'.
segments.s317-g88: بجنبد به گرسیوزش خون و مهر: "بجنبد" is a third‑person verb meaning 'will move / will be stirred'. "به" gives direction ('toward/against'). "گرسیوزش" is the proper name Garsivaz with the enclitic -ش (him), so the motion is directed at Garsivaz. "خون و مهر" ('blood and affection') are coordinated nouns describing the manner or accompaniment of the stirring — a poetic juxtaposition of violence and love. The clause says the heavens will be stirred against Garsivaz, with blood and mercy/affection.
segments.s317-g33: 'ز کوه اندر آوردمش تازیان' is a compact, somewhat inverted epic phrase: 'ز کوه' = 'from the mountain' (preposition before noun), 'اندر' here is the older/pre‑poetic locative particle folded into the phrase ('in/into' or part of the idiom 'from the mountain down'), 'آوردمش' is past first‑person 'I brought him' (‑م = object clitic 'him'), and 'تازیان' immediately follows as a nominative/identifying noun ('the Tazians/Arabs') that the poet places after the clause for gravity — syntactically you read it as two coordinated pieces: 'I brought him down from the mountain' and then 'the Tazians...'.
segments.s317-g34: This string lists two participial/agentive descriptors: 'خروشان' (present‑participle/adjective 'roaring, tumultuous') joined by 'و' to 'نوحه‌زنان' ('wailers, mourners'); 'چون' here is the simile 'like' and 'زنان' is 'women'. Grammatically, the whole phrase modifies or describes the Tazians (or their behavior) — 'the Tazians, roaring and wailing like women.'
segments.s317-g35: 'ز بس' is a causal/prepositional phrase 'from/ because of so much'; it governs the following coordinated nouns 'ناله و بانگ و سوگند' ('lamentation and cry/shout and oath') which are connected by و. The final 'اوی' is a possessive/pronoun attached to the list ('his'), so the whole segment means 'because of so much lamentation, shouting, and his oaths.'
segments.s317-g36: 'یکی سست کردم همی بند اوی' literally: 'I made/caused one (to be) loose — his bond.' 'یکی' = 'one (a single)', 'سست' is an adjective 'loose/unstable' used with کردن to mean 'to loosen', 'کردم' is the 1st‑person past 'I made/did', 'همی' is an older continuous/emphatic auxiliary often marking an ongoing/habitual or narrative past nuance, and 'بند اوی' = 'his fetter/bond'. Put together: 'I loosened one of his bonds.'
segments.s317-g37: 'بدین جایگه در ز چنگم بجست' begins with the deictic 'بدین جایگه' = 'at this place/at that very spot' (an older/pre‑poetic compound), then 'ز چنگم' = 'out of my grasp' (ز = 'from', چنگم = 'my grip' with possessive -م), and 'بجست' is past 3rd‑singular 'he sprang/leapt'. Word order places the location first for emphasis, then the source of escape, then the verb: 'At this spot he sprang from my grasp.'
segments.s317-g38: 'دل و جانم از رستن او بخست' is a compact result clause: 'دل و جانم' = 'my heart and my life/soul' (with the possessive suffix on جان implied by the previous m), 'از رستن او' = 'because of / at his escaping' ('رستن' = escape/freeing, 'او' = 'his/he'), and 'بخست' is a past/predicative meaning 'were shut/seized/struck' (here idiomatically 'were stricken' or 'stopped' by the fact of his escape). Together: 'my heart and life were stricken at his escape.'
segments.s317-g39: In 'بدین آب چیچست پنهان شدست' the opening 'بدین آب' is a topical/deictic phrase ('in/at this water' or 'with this water'), 'چیچست' is the interrogative 'what is it?', and 'پنهان شدست' = 'it has become hidden' (perfective/past passive sense). Word order is topical/question first, then the statement 'it is hidden.' The line reads as a question about something concealed in the water: 'What is this that is hidden in the water?'
segments.s317-g40: 'بگفتم ترا راست چونان که هست' is straightforward: 'بگفتم' = 'I said / I told', 'ترا' is the object 'to you' (here written as 'ت' + 'را' in the segmentation), 'راست' = 'truth/rightly' (= 'truthfully'), and the clause 'چونان که هست' = 'just as it is' or 'as it really is' (چونان = 'such/so', که هست = 'that is'). So: 'I told you the truth, just as it is.'
segments.s317-g17: بدو here is the prepositional clitic meaning “to him/unto him,” placed before the verb. گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (past) taking a direct speech; هوم‌ای is a vocative (personal name Hoom + -ای) addressing the addressee, and سرافراز مرد is a vocative noun phrase 'exalted/lofty man' apposed to the name.
segments.s317-g18: نگه کن is an imperative: نگه (watch/heed) + the imperative auxiliary/verb کن = 'take heed / stop / consider'. یکی is an indefinite 'one / a single (thing)' and اندر این کارکرد is a prepositional phrase (اندر = 'in/within') modifying which thing to heed — این کارکرد = 'this action/affair/deed'.
segments.s317-g19: یکی جای دارم is literally 'I have one/a place' with دارم as present 'I have' (the subject 'I' is implicit). بدین here functions as 'by/with this' (ب- + demonstrative), followed by تیغ کوه where تیغ 'sword' is linked to کوه 'mountain' (a noun-noun grouping: 'sword of the mountain' / 'mountain-sword'), describing the location or instrument.
segments.s317-g20: پرستشگه is a place-of-worship noun ('shrine/temple') serving as the location; بنده is used as humble self-reference ('I, your servant' or 'devotee') attached to that place, and دور از گروه is an adjectival/ adverbial phrase 'distant from the group' (دور = distant, followed by از + group). Together this yields 'a shrine where I, a humble worshipper, sit apart from the company.'
segments.s317-g21: شب تیره is a noun + adjective 'dark night' (adjective follows noun). بر پیش یزدان is a locative/scene phrase 'in the presence of / before the gods' (پیش = 'before, in the presence of'; بر is a prepositional particle). بدم here is an archaic first‑person form expressing the speaker's state or action during that time (contextually 'I spend / I pass / I am [in that state]'); the exact lexical sense is archaic/poetic.
segments.s317-g22: همه شب is an adverbial 'the whole night/all night.' ز یزدان پرستان is an ablative/causal phrase using poetic ز = 'from / because of' plus 'the god‑worshippers.' Again بدم repeats the archaic first‑person predicate indicating the speaker's afflicted condition or activity during the night; the exact lexical entry in the supplied senses list does not match perfectly, so it is here used idiomatically.
segments.s317-g23: بدان گه is a fixed temporal phrase 'at that time / at that moment' (بـ + دان/compound), followed by the subordinator که 'when/that' introducing the subordinate clause. خیزد is third-person singular present '(it) rises / begins,' and ز مرغان خروش is the source/agent phrase 'an uproar from the birds' (ز = 'from,' مرغان = 'birds,' خروش = 'uproar/cry').
segments.s317-g24: یکی ناله زارم is 'a single / a certain lament, my wail' (ناله = 'lament/cry', زارم = 'my woe / my plaintive cry' here used as a possessed noun). آمد is the simple past 'came,' and به گوش = 'to the ear' (idiomatically 'was heard'). The whole clause means 'one plaintive cry of mine came to the ear.'
segments.s317-g97: ببردند is a past plural verb (the archaising b‑ prefix + بردند) meaning 'they took/seized'. The noun phrase گرسیوز شوم را is the definite object: گرسیوز (name) modified postpositively by the adjective شوم ('ill‑omened'), with را marking the object/definite patient.
segments.s317-g98: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause ('for/that'), here giving the reason. آشوب is the noun 'turmoil' and از (poetic from) marks source ('from him'); او is the pronoun 'he'. بد functions as a predicative adjective 'evil/bad' and بر + بوم را ('upon the town/land' with را marking the definite affected place) indicates the target of the harm. The order is somewhat archaic but means 'because from him turmoil fell upon town and land'.
segments.s317-g99: به marks the indirect object ('to/for the torturer'), so به دژخیم فرمود means 'he commanded the torturer'. تا introduces the purpose/command ('so that / that ...'), and برکشید is the verb in the subordinate clause ('to lift / pull up'), here functioning as the action ordered (subjunctive/imperative sense after فرمود).
segments.s317-g100: ز is the poetic variant of از ('from'), so ز رخ پرده = 'the veil from the face'. پرده is 'veil/curtain' modified by شوم ('ill‑omened'); را marks that phrase as the definite object. بردرید is a past tense verb 'they tore apart' placed at the end in classical word order.
segments.s317-g101: همی is an adverbial particle often giving a continuous or narrative sense ('then/indeed/kept on'); دوخت is the past verb 'they sewed'. بر کتف او is a locative phrase 'on his shoulder' (بر + noun + pronoun). خام گاو is a noun phrase 'raw cow(‑hide)' with the adjective خام preceding the noun.
segments.s317-g102: چنین 'thus/so' introduces the result; تا here functions as a result/extent marker ('so that / until') and governs نماندش, a negative past verb nماند + enclitic pronoun ش = 'there remained to him/it not'. به تن = 'on the body' (locative) and هیچ تاو = 'no radiance' (تاو is an archaic/poetic noun 'radiance/glow'). The clause means 'so that no radiance remained on his body.'
segments.s317-g103: بر او پوست بدرید is literally 'they tore the skin upon him' (i.e. stripped his skin). The conjunction و links the next clause. زنهار is used as a noun here ('a plea/warning' → 'a plea for mercy') and خواست is the past verb 'he asked/sought' (the subject is the injured man). So 'and he begged for mercy.'
segments.s317-g104: جهان‌آفرین را is the object marked by را ('the Creator' as the thing he appeals to). همی is an adverbial particle (narrative/intonational), and یار خواست literally 'he sought (as) a companion/ally' — i.e. he begged that the Creator be his helper. خواست repeats as the main past verb governing this object.
segments.s317-g145: به خشکی is a prepositional phrase meaning 'toward/onto dry land' (به + noun). کشیدش is past tense 'pulled/drew' with the enclitic object -ش = 'him/it' attached; ز دریای آب ('from the sea of water') gives the source. Word order is poetic (destination phrase before the verb), but functionally: they pulled him (کشیدش) out of the sea (ز دریای آب) onto dry land (به خشکی).
segments.s317-g146: بشد is a past/resultative 'it happened/it became' here introducing the result; توش = 'in it/there' (referring to the dry land spot). و links clauses. هوش (sense) here means 'consciousness/wits' and از + رد افراسیاب indicates the agent/cause or context connected with Afrasiab. The clause reads 'there his senses left (he lost consciousness) because of/from Afrasiab' in poetic, compressed order.
segments.s317-g147: گرفته is a past participle/past verb 'seized' (here perfective), with ورا = 'to/at him' as its object following (archaic order). مرد دین‌دار is the subject 'the pious man' and دست 'hand' is the thing taken/held — poetic inversion places the verb phrase before the subject.
segments.s317-g148: به خواری 'to/into humiliation' marks the result or manner. ز دریا 'from the sea' marks the source. کشید is the past 'drew/dragged' (here used transitively) and و links the next verb. ببست is an older/epic past form meaning 'he bound/tied' (the doubled b- is an archaic epic prefix; functionally 'he closed/bound'). Together: 'he dragged him ashore in disgrace and bound him.'
segments.s317-g149: سپردش is past 'he entrusted/delivered him' with enclitic -ش = 'him'. بدیشان = 'to them' shows the recipient. و خود بازگشت puts the reflexive/emphatic خود before the verb بازگشت 'returned (he returned)'; the construction means 'and he himself returned.'
segments.s317-g150: تو گفتی 'you said' — گفتی is past second-person 'you said' and is followed by the complementizer که introducing the reported content. با باد here is a simile/means 'with/by the wind' (i.e. swiftly), انباز is an archaic/obscure adverb meaning something like 'swiftly' or 'at once', and گشت is past 'he went/returned' (used here as 'he returned'). The clause therefore reports: 'you said that he returned like the wind.'
segments.s317-g57: چنین گفت is a formulaic opening meaning “thus said.” In this classical word order the speech-act formula comes first, followed by the addressee/participants; با introduces the addressee here (classical use ≈ “to/with”), and کاووس شاه is the named speaker (name + title) placed after the introductory phrase.
segments.s317-g58: به یزدان سپاس is a short, fixed expression meaning “thanks/praise to God” where به marks the beneficiary. The conjunction و links a second imperative/optative clause; بدویم is the present-subjunctive/ hortative sense of دویدن here ("let us hasten/seek"), and پناه is its object “refuge” — together giving the sense “and let us seek refuge.”
segments.s317-g59: که introduces a content clause (“that/for”), and دیدم is simple past 1st‑person “I saw.” رخ (face/appearance) is the direct object; مرد (man/hero) and the postpositive compound یزدان‌پرست (God‑worshipper) are attributive modifiers to رخ (i.e. “the face of a God‑worshipping man”).
segments.s317-g60: توانا is an adjectival predicate meaning “powerful/able.” The coordinating و links further descriptive material: با + دانش is a prepositional phrase “with knowledge,” and the second و connects زور دست, a compact genitival phrase “strength (of) hand” (the ezāfe is omitted in poetic diction), i.e. ‘strong‑handed’ or ‘mighty in hand.’
segments.s317-g61: چنین داد پاسـخ is a narrative formula: داد (past of دادن) here means “gave” and takes پاسـخ “answer” as its object. پرستنده هوم is an appositive identification of the speaker — “Hoom, the worshipper” — following the verb and object (classical inversion is common).
segments.s317-g62: که begins the content of the answer (“that/that ...” or introduces a wish). آباد is an adjectival predicate “prosperous,” and بادا is an optative particle meaning “may it be.” به داد تو بوم is a prepositional + genitive phrase: به (by/through) + داد (justice) + تو (your) + بوم (land/homeland) — literally “may the land be prosperous by/through your justice.”
segments.s317-g63: بدین is a classical locution (≈ به این “to/for this”) introducing the beneficiary phrase; here it governs شاه (“for the king”). نوروز فرخنده is a noun + adjective “auspicious Nowruz,” and باد is the optative particle “may it be” (a common felicitation formula: “May this Nowruz be auspicious for the king”).
segments.s317-g64: دل is the subject “heart(s).” بدسگالان is a plural attributive meaning “ill‑omened/ill‑wishing ones” (i.e. enemies or ill‑wishers); او is the third‑person possessive reference “his.” کنده is a past participle “torn out/uprooted,” and باد (optative) closes the wish: “may the hearts of his ill‑omened ones be torn out.”
segments.s317-g65: پرستنده بودم is a predicate-nominal clause: پرستنده (a noun/adjective) is the predicative ('a worshipper/devotee') and بودم is the 1st-person past 'I was'. بدین کوهسار is a locative adjunct: بدین (classical/poetic 'به + این', 'at/to this') marks location and کوهسار ('mountain, highland') is the place. Word order reflects poetic inversion but means 'I was a devotee at this mountain.'
segments.s317-g66: که introduces a subordinate (here temporal/relative) clause ('when/that'). بگذشت is 3rd-person past 'passed by' (he passed). بر is the preposition governing the following noun (here indicating motion relative to the fortress: 'by/over/at'). گنگ qualifies دژ as 'silent/mute/muffled' and دژ is 'fortress'; شهریار names the agent/title 'the king/sovereign' who performs the passing.
segments.s317-g67: همی + خواستم expresses a continued or earnest past desire: همی gives a habitual/continuous flavour and خواستم is 1st-person past 'I wished/wanted'. تا introduces the content/purpose clause ('that/so that'), and جهان‌آفرین is the subject of that subordinate clause ('the world‑creator/Creator').
segments.s317-g68: بدو is the dative 'to him/for him' (indirect object). دارد is a present/past-base form here expressing the Creator's bringing/maintaining of a state (in classical style a present used for optative/result after تا). آباد is a predicative adjective 'prosperous/inhabited', and روی زمین is a locative expression 'on/upon the earth' (روی here functioning as 'upon/on' with زمین 'earth').
segments.s317-g69: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. باز آمد او means 'he came back/returned' (باز 'again' + آمد 'came') and the following شاد و خندان شدم is the speaker's result clause: شدم 'I became' plus the compound predicate adjectives شاد 'happy' and خندان 'smiling/cheerful' (joined by و).
segments.s317-g70: نیایش کنان is an adverbial/participial phrase: نیایش 'prayer' with the present-participle/gerundial suffix کنان 'doing', giving 'praying' or 'offering prayer'. پیش یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of God' and شدم again marks the speaker entering that state or position ('I became / I stood / I went before God').
segments.s317-g71: A noun phrase giving the agent: سروش (a proper name/angel) qualified by the adjective خجسته 'blessed/auspicious'. شبی 'one night' (adverbial of time) and ناگهان 'suddenly' further specify when and how the action occurred; word order here is poetic (agent + epithets + temporal/adverbial modifiers).
segments.s317-g72: بکرد is the 3rd-person past 'he did / he made' (causative/performative). آشکارا is an adverb 'openly / manifestly'. به من marks the experiencer/indirect object 'to me', and بر نهان denotes the direct object 'the hidden' (بر + نهان = 'that which was hidden' — literally 'upon the hidden', i.e. 'the hidden thing'). Together: 'he made the hidden manifest to me.'
segments.s317-g73: از این غار بی‌بن is a source phrase: از (preposition 'from') + این (demonstrative 'this') + غار (noun 'cave') modified by the adjective بی‌بن ('without root' / 'rootless'). برآمد is the simple past intransitive verb 'arose/issued forth.' خروش is the noun 'cry/roar' and appears after the verb — a poetic V‑then‑subject inversion common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s317-g74: شنیدم is the first‑person past 'I heard.' نهادم is another past verb 'I placed' (here transitive: 'I set/placed'), so the two past verbs share the same subject 'I.' به آواز گوش is a small prepositional phrase meaning '(my) ear to the voice/sound' (به = toward/into, آواز = 'voice/sound', گوش = 'ear'), together meaning 'I put my ear to the sound.'
segments.s317-g75: کسی is an indefinite subject 'someone.' زار here qualifies the manner of the action ('piteously' / 'in lament'), and بگریست is the past tense 'he/she wept' or 'wailed.' بر تخت عاج is a locative phrase 'upon the ivory throne' (بر = 'on/upon', تخت = 'throne', عاج = 'ivory'), placed after the verb as is common in elevated poetic word order.
segments.s317-g76: چه is an exclamatory/interrogative 'what' introducing a rhetorical question ('What has become of ...'). بر introduces the things affected ('on/to' the country etc.), and the list کشور و لشکر و تیغ و تاج strings together the affected items ('realm/country, army/host, sword, and crown'); the main verb is omitted here for rhetorical effect (elliptical: 'what [has happened] to...').
segments.s317-g77: ز is a poetic preposition equivalent to از ('from' or 'by/with'); here ز تیغ آمدم can be read as 'I came with/from the sword' (instrumental or ablative nuance). آمدم is the first‑person past 'I came,' and سوی آن غار تنگ is the directional phrase 'toward that narrow cave' (سوی = 'toward', آن = 'that', غار تنگ = 'narrow cave').
segments.s317-g78: This is a relative clause introduced by که ('which/that') modifying کمندی ('a snare/lasso'). زنار ('girdle') and بودم ('I was/there was for me') function together to express that the snare served as or was held like a girdle; بودم is a past form used here in a poetic sense of existence/possession ('I had/it was'). به چنگ is an adverbial phrase 'in (my) grasp/hand' (چنگ used metaphorically for a tight hold).
segments.s317-g79: بدیدم is the first‑person past 'I saw.' The direct object is the compound سر و گوش افراسیاب ('the head and ears of Afrasiab'), with the possessive name following the body parts — a normal Persian genitive construction where the possessor follows the possessed.
segments.s317-g80: در او ('in it') is a locative phrase (the pronoun likely picks up the nearby place/cave). ساخته is a past participle/past form 'made/was made' used predicatively, and جای آرام و خواب is the noun phrase 'a place of calm and sleep' (جای = 'place', آرام = 'calm/peace', خواب = 'sleep'); word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect but reads 'within it there was made a place of rest.'
segments.s317-g89: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. فرمان دهد is a verbal clause in the 3rd person (دهد) used in epic narrative as a past/performative 'gives/orders'; شهریار بلند is the subject phrase 'the lofty/high king' placed after the verb (VSO word order is common in Persian poetry).
segments.s317-g90: برادرش را is the definite object with the possessive enclitic -ش 'his'. پای کرده is a verbal compound: کرده is a past participle/auxiliary ('made/caused'), here meaning 'had ... put on (the feet)'. به بند is a prepositional/result phrase 'into fetters/fastened', completing the action.
segments.s317-g91: بیارند is a plural verb form meaning 'they bring/lay' (here an agentive 'they' is implicit); بر کتف او is a locative phrase 'on his shoulder' with او as the possessor. خام گاو is a noun phrase 'a raw/untamed cow' placed after the locative.
segments.s317-g92: بدوزند is the 3rd plural subjunctive/optative of دوختن 'they sew' (used as a narrative/polite form here); تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'in order that'), and گم کند is a subordinate present/subjunctive 'he may lose/lose' where کند is the auxiliary. زور و تاو are the coordinated objects 'strength and radiance'.
segments.s317-g93: چو is 'when'. آواز او is 'his voice/cry' (آواز = sound/cry, او = his). یابد is the verb 'finds/receives' (here used in the sense 'hears' or 'takes in'), and افراسیاب is the proper-name subject; Persian poetry permits this VSO-like ordering.
segments.s317-g94: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/behold'. برآید is the 3rd person verb 'he rises/appears'. ز دریای آب is a prepositional phrase 'from the sea (of water)' with ز (poetic 'from') plus دریای (construct form of دریا) and آب 'water'.
segments.s317-g95: بفرمود is the honorific/past narrative verb 'he commanded/ordered'. تا introduces the subordinate clause meaning 'that/so that' (introducing what was ordered). روزبانان is the plural subject 'the heralds', and در is the locative 'into/inside' which connects to the following verb.
segments.s317-g96: برفتند is the past/pluperfect narrative 'they went/entered' (3rd plural). با introduces instruments 'with', followed by تیغ 'sword' and گیلی سپر a noun phrase 'a Gilani (of Gilan) shield' (گیلی qualifying سپر); the coordination uses و 'and' between instruments.
segments.s317-g121: This clause begins with the conjunction که introducing a reported or explanatory clause ('that'). اکنون is an adverb 'now'. به دریا is a prepositional phrase 'to/for the sea' marking the object of need. نیاز آمدت is an impersonal/experiential expression: آمدت is the past/preterite with the second‑person enclitic -ت 'to you' and here means 'has come to you' (= 'you have'), so نیاز آمدت = 'a need has come to you' / 'you are in need'.
segments.s317-g122: چنین is a demonstrative/adverb 'thus/so' tying this clause to the previous statement. اختر 'star' is the noun subject. دیرساز is a compound adjective/noun 'one that makes/causes lateness' (here an ill‑omened or delay‑bringing star). آمدت repeats the same experiential construction as above ('has come to you' / 'has befallen you'), emphasizing the arrival of this ill omen.
segments.s317-g123: چو here is the temporal subordinating particle 'when'. بشنید is the past tense 'he heard'. بگریست is the past tense 'he wept' (ب‑ prefix is a classical marker often used before past/imperative forms in epic style). The proper name افراسیاب is postposed for poetic word order and is the subject doing both actions.
segments.s317-g124: همی is a classical continuous/habitual marker ('kept on / was repeatedly'), here giving an ongoing sense to ریخت (the past of ریختن 'to pour/let fall' or 'shed'). خونین modifies سرشک: خونین سرشک = 'blood‑red / blood‑stained teardrop(s)'. اندر آب is a prepositional phrase 'into the water'; overall: 'he kept shedding blood‑red tears into the water.'
segments.s317-g125: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase 'he gave such an answer' (چنین = 'thus', داد = 'gave', پاسخ = 'answer'). The conjunction که introduces the content of the reply. گرد جهان is a noun phrase 'around the world' (گرد = 'around / circuit', جهان = 'the world') and functions as the object/ground of the verb in the following clause.
segments.s317-g126: بگشتم is the speaker's verb 'I go about / I have gone about / I will go about' formed with the classical prefix بَـ plus the root (here indicating a habitual or iterative sense in epic narration). همی again marks continuing or habitual action. آشکار و نهان are adverbial/complementary opposites 'openly and hiddenly / manifest and secret' qualifying the manner of going about. (Note: the lemma/sense list supplied for بگشتم does not match this verb sense, so the sense index is given as unknown below.)
segments.s317-g127: کز is a causal/relative preposition 'from / because of / that from which'. این بخشش بد = 'this bad grant/forgiveness' (بخشش can mean both 'pardon' or 'gift' — here 'a bad act of granting/forgiveness'). مگر bگذرَم is idiomatic: here مگر introduces a rhetorical question ('could/should I') and بگذرم is first‑person singular 'I let it pass / I forgive / I pass over it' (optative/questioning sense). Together: 'Should I pass over this bad grant?'
segments.s317-g128: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of'. بد بدتر is a comparative phrase 'from bad (comes) worse' (بدتر = 'worse'). آمد is the past verb 'came / befell', کنون is the temporal adverb 'now', and بر سرم is a locative phrase 'upon my head' (بر = 'on/upon', سرم = 'my head'). So the clause means 'from bad, worse has now come upon my head.' Note: the supplied senses for سرم do not include 'head', so its sense is marked unknown below.
segments.s317-g129: مرا here is the dative/possessive construction (م + را) meaning 'for me' or 'my' (object/topic marking is split as م + را). زندگانی is the noun 'life' as the grammatical subject; کنون is a temporal adverb 'now'. خوار گشت is a predicate formed by the adjective خوار 'despised/worthless' plus گشت (past of گشتن) meaning 'became'. Word order is literal 'to-me life now despised became.'
segments.s317-g130: روانم is 'my soul/mind' (روان + enclitic -م). پر از is the light adjectival phrase 'full of' (پر followed by the preposition از). درد و تیمار are coordinated nouns 'pain and care/worry'; تیمار here carries the sense of solicitude/anxious care. گشت again is 'became', so the clause says 'my soul became full of pain and care.'
segments.s317-g131: نبیره فریدون و پور پشنگ names two persons by genealogy: نبیره 'offspring/scion' of Fereydun, و linking to پور 'son/descendant' of Pashang. These are noun phrases naming the people involved; پور is used in the literal sense 'son/descendant' rather than merely an element of a name.
segments.s317-g132: برآویخته is a past/passive participial/adjectival form meaning 'lifted/raised' (lit. 'thrown up'). سر is the noun 'head'. به here marks direction/goal ('toward/into'), and کام نهنگ functions idiomatically as 'the whale's maw' (کام literally 'satisfaction'/'mouth' in poetic usage). The phrase reads 'their heads raised toward/into the whale's maw.'
segments.s317-g133: همی is a continuous/iterative particle 'continually/still'. پوست here refers to 'hide/skin' (the material), درند is a present/plural verb form 'they rend/tear' (poetic present), بر وی means 'on him', and به چرم indicates result 'into leather'. The clause literally: 'They keep tearing his skin on him into leather.'
segments.s317-g134: کسی را نبینم is a negative first-person clause where نبینم is the negative subjunctive/optative form used as 'I see no one' (poetic usage). را is the object marker attached to کسی. به چشم here uses به with the instrumentive/figurative sense 'with/by (the) eye(s)' and چشم آب شرم is a genitival phrase 'eyes of water (i.e. tears) of shame' — 'eyes wet with shameful tears.'
segments.s317-g135: زبان here means 'speech/utterance' (not just the physical tongue). دو مهتر 'the two chiefs/elders' is the possessed/associated noun, and پر از گفتگوی is the adjectival phrase 'full of conversation' (پر 'full' + از 'of' + گفتگوی 'conversation'). Word order: 'speech of the two chiefs full of conversation.'
segments.s317-g136: روان پرستنده reads as 'the worshipper's spirit/soul' (روان 'soul' + پرستنده 'worshipper/devotee'); پر جستجوی is 'full of seeking/search' (again the adjective پر 'full' modifying جستجوی 'search'). The phrase describes an inner state: 'the worshipper's spirit is full of questing/yearning.'
segments.s317-g137: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. یزدان پرستنده is a poetic compound meaning 'God-worshipper' or 'the God-fearing man' (adjective+noun order inverted for style). او را is the definite-object phrase ('him' + را) placed before the verb, and بدید is the past-tense 3rd-person singular of دیدن ('saw'). The clause sets the time frame for what follows.
segments.s317-g138: چنان is an intensifying adverb 'such/so'; نوحه زار is a noun phrase 'a plaintive/woeful lament' where زار functions adjectivally ('piteous/plaintive') modifying نوحه. ایشان is a genitive/possessive source ('their') attached without the expected ezâfe in this poetic line, and شنید is past 'he heard'. The whole segment describes what he heard from them.
segments.s317-g139: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; راه جزیره is a genitive phrase 'the road/way of the island'; برآمد is the past 'emerged/appeared'; and یکی (here after the verb) is an indefinite 'one'/'someone' identifying the newcomer. Word order (prepositional phrase + verb + indefinite) is typical of epic poetry.
segments.s317-g140: چو again = 'when'. دیدش is 'saw him' (دید + enclitic object -ش). مر is an archaic/dialectal element indicating motion/tendency toward the speaker or addressee (used here to signal the newcomer's approach), and او را is the explicit object 'him' (poetic order). ز دور اندکی is an adverbial phrase 'from a short/near distance' modifying the approach. Together the clause says that, on seeing him, the newcomer came a little from afar.
segments.s317-g141: گشاد is a past form of گشودن 'opened/loosed' (here a transitive past). آن کیانی کمند is a noun phrase 'that Kayanian/royal lasso' (آن demonstrative + کیانی attributive + کمند noun). از میان is a prepositional phrase 'out of the midst/from between' indicating where the lasso came from or was loosed.
segments.s317-g142: دو تایی is an adjective 'twofold / in pair' describing what emerged; بیامد is past 'came forth'. چو is a simile marker 'like/as', and شیر ژیان is a compound 'living/fierce lion' (شیر 'lion' + ژیان 'life' → 'living/lifelike', i.e. fierce). The clause uses a simile to compare the emerging parts to a lion.
segments.s317-g143: بینداخت is the past of انداختن 'to throw/cast' (he threw). آن گرد کرده کمند is the object phrase 'that round/coiled lasso' where گرد کرده is a participial modifier ('made round/coiled') attached to کمند, and آن is the demonstrative. The verb precedes its object in this poetic line.
segments.s317-g144: سر شهریار is a genitive noun phrase 'the head of the king' (poetic omission of ezâfe). اندر آمد is a verbal phrase 'entered / came into' (here passive/resultative: was brought into), and به بند means 'into the bond/fetter' — together describing that the king's head became caught in the noose.

sentences.s317-s25: At that moment the kings mounted their horses and rode off from the iwan of Azar‑Gushasp.
sentences.s317-s26: The king grew thoughtful because of that speech, and (he) came near the pious one.
sentences.s317-s27: When Hom saw that head and crown of the kings, he showered upon them the acclamation for justice.
sentences.s317-s28: All the kings praised him, continually chanting, "Praise be to the Creator of the World."
sentences.s317-s21: When Goudarz heard this story, the truthful words came to his mind.
sentences.s317-s22: From there he went toward the fire-temple, for the people were distraught.
sentences.s317-s23: First he offered praise to the fire; he offered prayer to the Creator.
sentences.s317-s24: He then set about and revealed the hidden secret; he told the kings what he had seen.
sentences.s317-s1: It so happened that Goudarz, leader of the keshvadegan, was going with Giv and the nobles.
sentences.s317-s2: The boars and the pursuers drew near the king and for a while fixed their gaze toward the sea.
sentences.s317-s3: To his eye appeared Hum with that lasso; a youth on the water's brink (aiming) toward the needy.
sentences.s317-s4: In the same manner he saw the water dark; he saw the worshipper, his eyes fixed and staring.
sentences.s317-s57: He cried aloud, "O king of the world, head of the renowned and crown of the great!"
sentences.s317-s58: Whence came all that custom and ceremony and those occasions? Whence came that head, that crown, and so many hosts?
sentences.s317-s59: Whence came all that learning and strength of hand? Whence came those great ones who worshipped Khosrow?
sentences.s317-s60: Whence came that glory and name within the field of battle? Whence came that pleasure and cup within the banquet-hall?
sentences.s317-s53: When Afrasiab heard his voice, full of sorrow he rose from the water weeping.
sentences.s317-s54: His companion kept setting foot toward the sea and came to a place that was a poor footing.
sentences.s317-s55: When he heard his brother's cry from the dry land, what he saw struck him as worse than death.
sentences.s317-s56: When Garsivaz saw him in the water, his two eyes were full of blood and his heart full of haste.
sentences.s317-s5: He thought to himself, "What prey can this pious man take from the sea?"
sentences.s317-s6: Perhaps a whale had seized the fish's tail; at the sight of him he remained in amazement.
sentences.s317-s7: He said to him, 'O pious man, what secret do you have? Make it manifest.'
sentences.s317-s8: What do you seek from this sea of water? Do you perhaps hope to wash your dark body?
sentences.s317-s13: At that moment a thought seized my clear heart: that I would uproot the root of vengeance from the world.
sentences.s317-s14: Such a sound at bedtime ought not to be (anyone's) except Afrasiab.
sentences.s317-s15: I took to searching every mountain and cave; I saw, in that moment, the mourner.
sentences.s317-s16: I bound his two hands with a girdle like a stone, so that his two claws were blood‑stained.
sentences.s317-s41: I fastened his snare with a bond; like a stone I hauled him out, the poor wretch, from that narrow place.
sentences.s317-s42: At his request I slackened the snare; when it reached the water the fetter loosened.
sentences.s317-s43: He is now hidden in the water; whoever follows him must leave the world.
sentences.s317-s44: But if victory is regained, the heavens will stir against Garsivaz with blood and affection.
sentences.s317-s17: I brought him down from the mountain; the Tazians roaring, and wailers like women.
sentences.s317-s18: Because of so much lamentation, shouting, and his oaths, I loosened one of his bonds.
sentences.s317-s19: At that very spot he leapt from my grasp; my heart and life were stricken by his escape.
sentences.s317-s20: What is this hidden in the water? I told you truly, just as it is.
sentences.s317-s9: He said to him, “Hoom — O exalted man, stop and heed one thing in this affair.”
sentences.s317-s10: I have a place — by this sword on the mountain; a shrine where I, a humble worshipper, am apart from the company.
sentences.s317-s11: I spend the dark night before the gods; all night I am afflicted because of the god‑worshippers.
sentences.s317-s12: At that moment when an uproar rose among the birds, a single plaintive cry of mine came to the ear.
sentences.s317-s49: They seized the ill‑omened Garsivaz, for from him a turmoil fell grievously upon the towns and the land.
sentences.s317-s50: He ordered the torturer to lift (him) up; they tore the ill‑omened one's veil from his face.
sentences.s317-s51: They sewed a raw cowhide on his shoulder thus, so that no radiance remained on his body.
sentences.s317-s52: They stripped the skin from him, and he cried for mercy; he implored the Creator to be his ally.
sentences.s317-s73: They pulled him from the sea onto dry land; there Afrasiab lost consciousness.
sentences.s317-s74: A pious man seized him by the hand; he dragged him ashore in disgrace from the sea and bound him.
sentences.s317-s75: He delivered him to them and himself returned; you said that he returned like the wind.
sentences.s317-s29: Kavus the king thus said to Hoom: "Praise be to God, and let us seek refuge."
sentences.s317-s30: for I saw the face of a God-worshipping man, powerful and endowed with knowledge and brute strength of hand.
sentences.s317-s31: Thus replied Hoom the worshipper: "May your land be prosperous through your justice."
sentences.s317-s32: May this Nowruz be auspicious for the king; may the hearts of his ill‑omened foes be torn out.
sentences.s317-s33: I was a devotee on this mountain when the sovereign passed by the silent fortress.
sentences.s317-s34: I wished that the Creator would make the face of the earth prosperous for him.
sentences.s317-s35: When he returned I became joyful and smiling; doing prayer I stood before God.
sentences.s317-s36: The blessed Soroush (angel), one night, suddenly made the hidden manifest to me openly.
sentences.s317-s37: From this rootless cave a cry arose; I heard it and pressed my ear to the sound.
sentences.s317-s38: Someone wailed piteously upon the ivory throne, crying, 'What has become of the realm and the host, the sword and the crown?'
sentences.s317-s39: With sword in hand I came toward that narrow cave; a snare — which I held like a girdle in my grasp.
sentences.s317-s40: I saw Afrasiab's head and ears; inside it a place of calm and sleep had been made.
sentences.s317-s45: When the lofty king gave the order, he had his brother's feet put in fetters.
sentences.s317-s46: They laid a raw cow upon his shoulder and sewed it on so that he would lose his strength and radiance.
sentences.s317-s47: When Afrasiab hears his cry, verily he rises from the sea.
sentences.s317-s48: He commanded that the heralds go in with sword and a Gilani shield.
sentences.s317-s61: That now you are in need of the sea; such a delay‑bringing star has come upon you.
sentences.s317-s62: When he heard, Afrasiab wept; blood‑red teardrops poured into the water.
sentences.s317-s63: He answered thus: 'I go about the world, both openly and in secret.'
sentences.s317-s64: Shall I let this evil grant pass? From bad, something worse has now come upon my head.
sentences.s317-s65: My life has now become despised; my spirit is filled with pain and anxious care.
sentences.s317-s66: A scion of Fereydun and the son of Pashang had their heads raised toward the whale's maw.
sentences.s317-s67: They are tearing his skin into leather; I see no one with eyes wet from tears of shame.
sentences.s317-s68: The speech of the two chiefs is full of conversation; the worshipper's spirit is full of seeking.
sentences.s317-s69: When the God-fearing man saw him, he heard such a plaintive lament from them.
sentences.s317-s70: From the island's road came one; when he saw him, he approached from a short distance.
sentences.s317-s71: He loosed that Kayanian lasso from the middle; the twofold parts came forth like a living lion.
sentences.s317-s72: He cast that coiled lasso; the king's head came into the fetter.
